perturbation.original_text: In the event Chemsultants is requested by applicable law or regulation or by legal process to disclose any Confidential Information or any other information concerning Client, Chemsultants agrees that it will provide Client with prompt notice of such request or requirement in order to enable Client to seek an appropriate protective order or other remedy, to consult with Client with respect to taking steps to resist or narrow the scope of such request or legal process, or to waive compliance, in whole or in part, with the terms of this Agreement. In any such event Chemsultants will use its reasonable best efforts to ensure that all Confidential Information and other information that is so disclosed will be accorded confidential treatment.
perturbation.changed_text: In any such event Chemsultants will use its reasonable best efforts to ensure that all Confidential Information and other information that is so disclosed will be accorded confidential treatment. In the event Chemsultants is requested by applicable law or regulation or by legal process to disclose any Confidential Information or any other information concerning Client, Chemsultants agrees that it will provide Client with prompt notice of such request or requirement in order to enable Client to seek an appropriate protective order or other remedy, to consult with Client with respect to taking steps to resist or narrow the scope of such request or legal process, or to waive compliance, in whole or in part, with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By inverting the order of the sentences in Section 3, the required action to 'accord confidential treatment' occurs before there is an obligation to notify the client or seek a protective order. This may hinder the client's ability to properly protect their confidential information, as the confidential treatment is prioritized over notification and legal remedies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act, 18 U.S. Code § 1839
perturbation.law_explanation: The change could impede appropriate legal recourse under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). The DTSA provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation, and the client's ability to seek injunctive relief or damages could be compromised if they aren't promptly notified and able to take steps to protect their information before disclosure occurs. By prioritizing immediate confidential treatment without prior notice, the modification potentially weakens the client's ability to leverage the DTSA's protections effectively.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the intent of the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), as explained by the LLM. By prioritizing immediate confidential treatment over prompt notification, the client's ability to leverage the DTSA's protections is weakened, thereby creating a legal contradiction. While the snippets primarily define terms such as "trade secret" and "misappropriation," the LLM explanation states the essence of DTSA is to give a cause of action when trade secrets are misappropriated. The altered contract hinders the client's recourse under DTSA, because the original order of actions – notifying the client before any confidential treatment – gave the client a chance to seek a protective order or other remedy. Now, the client is notified later in the process, weakening that ability.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, construed, and enforced in accordance with the laws, and in the courts, of the State of Ohio, County of Lake, without giving effect to any conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, construed, and enforced in accordance with the laws, and in the courts, of the State of Ohio, County of Lake, with giving effect to any conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'without giving effect' to 'with giving effect', the conflict of laws principles will be applied, and the law applied to the case may be the law of a different jurisdiction depending on the circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: By adding the phrase 'with giving effect to any conflict of laws principles', this contradicts Ohio Revised Code § 1.58 which outlines the law that needs to be applied without regard to conflict of laws, potentially creating confusion in the legal jurisdiction that is to be followed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1.58 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1.58 | Reenactment, amendment, or repeal of statute. Ohio Revised Code / General Provisions / Chapter 1 Definitions; Rules of Construction Effective: January 3, 1972 Latest Legislation: House Bill 607 - 109th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) The reenactment, amendment, or repeal of a statute does not, except as provided in division (B) of this section: (1) Affect the prior operation of the statute or any prior action taken thereunder; (2) Affect any validation, cure, right, privilege, obligation, or liability previously acquired, accrued, accorded, or incurred thereunder; (3) Affect any violation thereof or penalty, forfeiture, or punishment incurred in respect thereto, prior to the amendment or repeal; (4) Affect any investigation, proceeding, or remedy in respect of any such privilege, obligation, liability, penalty, forfeiture, or punishment; and the investigation, proceeding, or remedy may be instituted, continued, or enforced, and the penalty, forfeiture, or punishment imposed, as if the statute had not been repealed or amended. (B) If the penalty, forfeiture, or punishment for any offense is reduced by a reenactment or amendment of a statute, the penalty, forfeiture, or punishment, if not already imposed, shall be imposed according to the statute as amended. Available Versions of this Section January 3, 1972 – House Bill 607 - 109th General Assembly [ View January 3, 1972 Version ]

perturbation.justification: The change from 'without giving effect' to 'with giving effect' to conflict of laws principles directly contradicts the original intent of applying Ohio law without considering the laws of other jurisdictions. The LLM Law Explanation states that this change contradicts Ohio Revised Code § 1.58, which outlines the law that needs to be applied without regard to conflict of laws, potentially creating confusion in the legal jurisdiction. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (Section 1.58 - Ohio Revised Code) doesn't explicitly prohibit considering conflict of laws, it does reinforce that Ohio law should be the primary law applied. By introducing conflict of laws principles, the changed text weakens the direct application of Ohio law, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement contains the entire agreement between the parties concerning the confidentiality of the Confidential Information and cannot be modified, released, discharged, abandoned, or otherwise terminated in whole or in part unless approved in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement contains the entire agreement between the parties concerning the confidentiality of the Confidential Information and cannot be modified, released, discharged, abandoned, or otherwise terminated in whole or in part unless approved in writing; unless such approval can be reasonably implied.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the phrase 'unless such approval can be reasonably implied', a verbal agreement could be misconstrued by either party. The original text requires the approval to be explicitly written.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ohio Statute of Frauds, Ohio Revised Code § 1335.05
perturbation.law_explanation: The added phrase 'unless such approval can be reasonably implied' creates ambiguity in the agreement's modification clause, contradicting the intention of the Statute of Frauds and allowing for possible disputes over modifications without written consent. This would allow a breach of contract to occur more easily, as proving 'reasonable implication' is subjective and could weaken the enforceability of the original written agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1335.05 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1335.05 | Certain agreements to be in writing. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1335 Statute Of Frauds Effective: July 1, 1976 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1426 - 111th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF No action shall be brought whereby to charge the defendant, upon a special promise, to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another person; nor to charge an executor or administrator upon a special promise to answer damages out of his own estate; nor to charge a person upon an agreement made upon consideration of marriage, or upon a contract or sale of lands, tenements, or hereditaments, or interest in or concerning them, or upon an agreement that is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof; unless the agreement upon which such action is brought, or some memorandum or note thereof, is in writing and signed by the party to be charged therewith or some other person thereunto by him or her lawfully authorized. No action shall be brought to charge a person licensed by Chapter 4731. of the Revised Code to practice medicine or surgery, osteopathic medicine or surgery, or podiatric medicine and surgery in this state, upon any promise or agreement relating to a medical prognosis unless the promise or agreement is in writing and signed by the party to be charged therewith. Available Versions of this Section July 1, 1976 – House Bill 1426 - 111th General Assembly [ View July 1, 1976 Version ]

perturbation.justification: The addition of 'unless such approval can be reasonably implied' directly contradicts the original agreement's intent that modifications must be in writing. This creates ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of the original agreement, as stated in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address contract modification clauses, it emphasizes the need for certain agreements to be in writing. Allowing implied approval opens the door for disputes and weakens the requirement for written consent, creating a structural flaw that can lead to legal contradictions.
